OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners

Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Divergences and Dual Norms

This chapter compares optimal transport with divergence-based and adversarial ways of measuring discrepancy. The main stake is topological: ϕ\phiϕ-divergences are cheap but strong, while dual norms and GAN objectives can be weak enough to compare singular measures. The discussion connects classical information divergences Ciszár, 1967Ali & Silvey, 1966 with modern integral probability metrics and generative modeling Sriperumbudur et al., 2009Goodfellow et al., 2014Arjovsky et al., 2017.

Guiding Comparison

Dual norms and integral probability metrics compare measures through test functions. Phi-divergences compare them through density ratios. This single change explains most of the chapter: test-function discrepancies can be weak enough to compare singular measures, while ratio-based divergences are often cheap and statistically classical but topologically strong.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Dual Norms and Integral Probability Metrics¶

Dual norms generalize the W1\Wass_1W1​ test-function principle. They are useful in statistics because they compare distributions by restricting the discriminator class.

Integral Probability Metrics¶

The Kantorovich--Rubinstein formula for W1\Wass_1W1​ is a special case of a dual norm. This viewpoint designs weak discrepancies by testing signed differences of measures against a controlled class of functions.

Definition: Dual Norm and Integral Probability Metric

For a symmetric convex set BBB of measurable functions, define on signed measures ξ\xiξ

∥ξ∥B:=sup⁡f∈B∫Xf(x)  dξ(x).\norm{\xi}_B \eqdef \sup_{f\in B} \int_\X f(x)\,\d\xi(x).∥ξ∥B​:=f∈Bsup​∫X​f(x)dξ(x).
(1)

When this quantity is applied to α−β\alpha-\betaα−β for probability measures, it is often called an integral probability metric.

The choice of the test-function class BBB determines both the topology and the statistical behavior of the discrepancy Sriperumbudur et al., 2012Sriperumbudur et al., 2009Sriperumbudur et al., 2008.

Example: Total variation

As recalled in Definition Definition: Total Variation and Proposition Proposition: Dual And Measure Definitions Of Total Variation, total variation is the dual norm associated with the unit ball of continuous functions

B={f∈C(X)  :  ∥f∥∞≤1}.B = \enscond{f \in \Cc(\X)}{\norm{f}_\infty \leq 1}.B={f∈C(X):∥f∥∞​≤1}.
(2)

Total variation is the canonical nontrivial example of a discrepancy that is both a ϕ\phiϕ-divergence and a dual norm; see Sriperumbudur et al., 2009.

Example: W1\Wass_1W1​ norm

W1\Wass_1W1​, as defined in (31), is the dual norm (1) associated with

B={f  :  Lip⁡(f)≤1}B = \enscond{f}{\Lip(f) \leq 1}B={f:Lip(f)≤1}
(3)

the set of 1-Lipschitz functions.

Example: Flat norm and Dudley metric

If the set BBB is bounded and separates measures, then ∥⋅∥B\norm{\cdot}_B∥⋅∥B​ is a norm on the whole space M(X)\Mm(\Xx)M(X) of finite measures.

This is not the case of W1\Wass_1W1​, which is only finite on signed measures ξ\xiξ such that ∫X dξ=0\int_\X \d\xi=0∫X​dξ=0; otherwise ∥ξ∥B=+∞\norm{\xi}_B=+\infty∥ξ∥B​=+∞ because constants belong to the Lipschitz ball.

This is remedied by imposing a bound on the value of the potential f\ff, which leads for instance to the flat norm,

B={f  :  Lip⁡(f)≤1and∥f∥∞≤1}.B=\enscond{f}{\Lip(f) \leq 1 \qquad\text{and}\qquad \norm{\f}_\infty \leq 1}.B={f:Lip(f)≤1and∥f∥∞​≤1}.
(4)

On compact metric spaces, it metrizes weak convergence on the whole space M(X)\Mm(\X)M(X) of finite measures.

The finite-dimensional version is obtained from the usual W1\Wass_1W1​ dual linear program by adding the box constraints ∣fk∣≤1\abs{\fD_k}\leq1∣fk​∣≤1.

The flat norm is sometimes called the “Kantorovich--Rubinstein” norm Hanin, 1992 and has been used as a fidelity term for inverse problems in imaging Lellmann et al., 2014.

The flat norm is similar to the Dudley metric, which uses

B={f  :  ∥∇f∥∞+∥f∥∞≤1}.B=\enscond{f}{\norm{\nabla \f}_\infty + \norm{\f}_\infty \leq 1}.B={f:∥∇f∥∞​+∥f∥∞​≤1}.
(5)
show_book_figure("dualnorms-ipm-witnesses")

Dual witnesses for integral probability metrics. The red and blue curves are two one-dimensional probability densities and the violet curve is a normalized optimal dual witness fα,β⋆f^\star_{\alpha,\beta}fα,β⋆​ for the IPM variational problem. W1\Wass_1W1​ restricts the slope through Kantorovich--Rubinstein duality, MMD restricts the RKHS norm, and total variation can saturate pointwise and therefore reacts sharply to signed density differences.

The interactive demo makes the topology visible. As the two densities move, the total-variation witness jumps with the sign of the density difference, the Wasserstein witness keeps a unit-slope geometry, and the MMD witness is smoothed by the kernel bandwidth.

Interactive panel. Use the kernel, bandwidth, and separation controls to see how witness functions detect differences between measures.

The following proposition gives a compact-space criterion. The dual ball should be rich enough to approximate continuous observables, but compact enough for weak convergence to imply uniform convergence over the discriminator class.

Proposition: Metrization by Dual Norms

Assume that X\XX is compact, B=−BB=-BB=−B, and the measures considered are probability measures.

  1. If every function in C(X)\Cc(\X)C(X) can be uniformly approximated by elements of span⁡(B)\operatorname{span}(B)span(B), then ∥αn−α∥B→0\norm{\alpha_n-\alpha}_B\to0∥αn​−α∥B​→0 implies αn⇀α\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaαn​⇀α.

  2. If B⊂C(X)B\subset\Cc(\X)B⊂C(X) is compact for ∥⋅∥∞\norm{\cdot}_\infty∥⋅∥∞​, then αn⇀α\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaαn​⇀α implies ∥αn−α∥B→0\norm{\alpha_n-\alpha}_B\to0∥αn​−α∥B​→0.

Proof

For the first implication, ∥αn−α∥B→0\norm{\alpha_n-\alpha}_B\to0∥αn​−α∥B​→0 and the symmetry of BBB imply

∣∫f  d(αn−α)∣≤∥αn−α∥B(f∈B).\left|\int f\,\d(\alpha_n-\alpha)\right| \le \norm{\alpha_n-\alpha}_B \qquad (f\in B).∣∣​∫fd(αn​−α)∣∣​≤∥αn​−α∥B​(f∈B).
(6)

By linearity, integrals converge for every h∈span⁡(B)h\in\operatorname{span}(B)h∈span(B). Let u∈C(X)u\in\Cc(\X)u∈C(X) and choose h∈span⁡(B)h\in\operatorname{span}(B)h∈span(B) with ∥u−h∥∞≤η\norm{u-h}_\infty\le\eta∥u−h∥∞​≤η. Since αn\alpha_nαn​ and α\alphaα are probabilities,

∣∫u  d(αn−α)∣≤∣∫h  d(αn−α)∣+2η.\left|\int u\,\d(\alpha_n-\alpha)\right| \le \left|\int h\,\d(\alpha_n-\alpha)\right| +2\eta .∣∣​∫ud(αn​−α)∣∣​≤∣∣​∫hd(αn​−α)∣∣​+2η.
(7)

Taking the limsup as n→∞n\to\inftyn→∞ and then letting η→0\eta\to0η→0 gives weak convergence.

For the second implication, assume αn⇀α\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaαn​⇀α and choose a subsequence (αnk)k(\alpha_{n_k})_k(αnk​​)k​ realizing the limsup of ∥αn−α∥B\norm{\alpha_n-\alpha}_B∥αn​−α∥B​. Since BBB is compact and f↦∫f  d(αnk−α)f\mapsto\int f\,\d(\alpha_{n_k}-\alpha)f↦∫fd(αnk​​−α) is continuous on BBB, the supremum is attained by some fnk∈Bf_{n_k}\in Bfnk​​∈B. Extract a further subsequence with fnk→ff_{n_k}\to ffnk​​→f uniformly. Then

∫fnk  d(αnk−α)=∫f  d(αnk−α)+∫(fnk−f)  dαnk−∫(fnk−f)  dα.\int f_{n_k}\,\d(\alpha_{n_k}-\alpha) = \int f\,\d(\alpha_{n_k}-\alpha) + \int (f_{n_k}-f)\,\d\alpha_{n_k} - \int (f_{n_k}-f)\,\d\alpha .∫fnk​​d(αnk​​−α)=∫fd(αnk​​−α)+∫(fnk​​−f)dαnk​​−∫(fnk​​−f)dα.
(8)

The first term tends to zero by weak convergence and the last two by uniform convergence. Hence the limsup is zero.

Corollary: Wasserstein Metrizes Weak Convergence

On a compact metric space, Wp\Wass_pWp​ metrizes weak convergence on probability measures for every p≥1p\ge1p≥1.

Proof

For p=1p=1p=1, take B={f:Lip⁡(f)≤1}B=\{f:\operatorname{Lip}(f)\le1\}B={f:Lip(f)≤1}. The span of BBB contains all Lipschitz functions, which are dense in C(X)\Cc(\X)C(X) on compact metric spaces. This gives W1(αn,α)→0⇒αn⇀α\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\alpha)\to0\Rightarrow\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaW1​(αn​,α)→0⇒αn​⇀α.

Conversely, constants do not change the pairing with αn−α\alpha_n-\alphaαn​−α. Fix x0∈Xx_0\in\Xx0​∈X and normalize potentials by f(x0)=0f(x_0)=0f(x0​)=0. The normalized unit Lipschitz ball is uniformly bounded by diam⁡(X)\operatorname{diam}(\X)diam(X) and equicontinuous, hence compact in ∥⋅∥∞\norm{\cdot}_\infty∥⋅∥∞​ by Arzela--Ascoli. The previous proposition gives W1(αn,α)→0\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\alpha)\to0W1​(αn​,α)→0. On compact spaces, all Wp\Wass_pWp​ distances induce the same topology.

Dual RKHS Norms and Maximum Mean Discrepancies¶

Kernel methods turn probability measures into mean elements of a reproducing kernel Hilbert space. The resulting Hilbertian dual norms are quadratic discrepancies, handled with Euclidean geometry while retaining a weak test-function interpretation.

Definition: Positive and Conditionally Positive Kernels

A symmetric function K:X×X→RK:\X\times\X\to\RRK:X×X→R is positive definite if for every n≥1n\ge1n≥1, every x1,…,xn∈Xx_1,\ldots,x_n\in\Xx1​,…,xn​∈X, and every r∈Rnr\in\RR^nr∈Rn,

∑i,j=1nrirjK(xi,xj)≥0.\sum_{i,j=1}^n r_i r_j K(x_i,x_j)\ge0.i,j=1∑n​ri​rj​K(xi​,xj​)≥0.
(9)

It is conditionally positive definite if the same inequality is required only for zero-sum vectors, ⟨r,1⟩=0\langle r,\mathbf{1}\rangle=0⟨r,1⟩=0.

The conditional version is the right notion for probability distances, because one applies the quadratic form to signed measures ξ=α−β\xi=\alpha-\betaξ=α−β of total mass zero. Adding a(x)+a(y)a(x)+a(y)a(x)+a(y) to the kernel does not change ∬K(x,y)  dξ(x)  dξ(y)\iint K(x,y)\,\d\xi(x)\,\d\xi(y)∬K(x,y)dξ(x)dξ(y) on such measures, and many natural distance kernels are only conditionally positive definite.

Example: Riesz, energy and Mat'ern-type kernels

On Rd\RR^dRd, translation-invariant kernels are most transparent in Fourier variables. The Riesz family associated with (−Δ)−s(-\Delta)^{-s}(−Δ)−s has multiplier ∥ω∥−2s\norm{\om}^{-2s}∥ω∥−2s and defines a nonnegative quadratic form on zero-mass measures for which the low-frequency singularity is integrable; this is the kernel counterpart of classical Riesz potentials Berg et al., 1984. The energy distance corresponds to the conditionally positive kernel k(x,y)=−∥x−y∥\Krkhs(x,y)=-\norm{x-y}k(x,y)=−∥x−y∥, whose Fourier multiplier is proportional to ∥ω∥−(d+1)\norm{\om}^{-(d+1)}∥ω∥−(d+1); for ξ=α−β\xi=\al-\beξ=α−β,

−∬∥x−y∥ dξ(x) dξ(y)-\iint \norm{x-y}\d\xi(x)\d\xi(y)−∬∥x−y∥dξ(x)dξ(y)
(10)

is the squared energy distance up to a dimensional constant Schoenberg, 1938Székely & Rizzo, 2004.

Shifted kernels replace (−Δ)−s(-\Delta)^{-s}(−Δ)−s by (−Δ+λI)−s(-\Delta+\lambda I)^{-s}(−Δ+λI)−s with λ>0\lambda>0λ>0. Their Fourier multiplier (∥ω∥2+λ)−s(\norm{\om}^2+\lambda)^{-s}(∥ω∥2+λ)−s is bounded at the origin, hence the kernel is positive definite without imposing zero mass. These are Matern kernels; in closed form they are radial and involve a modified Bessel function Wendland, 2005. The Laplacian kernel e−∥x−y∥/σe^{-\norm{x-y}/\sigma}e−∥x−y∥/σ is a low-smoothness Matern example, while the Gaussian kernel e−∥x−y∥2/(2σ2)e^{-\norm{x-y}^2/(2\sigma^2)}e−∥x−y∥2/(2σ2) is the infinite-smoothness limit after the usual rescaling of the Matern smoothness parameter.

Definition: Kernel Norm and MMD

Let KKK be positive definite. More generally, let KKK be conditionally positive definite and restrict attention to signed measures of total mass zero. For a signed measure ξ\xiξ with finite kernel energy, define

∥ξ∥K2:=∬X×XK(x,y)  dξ(x)  dξ(y).\norm{\xi}_K^2 \eqdef \iint_{\X\times\X}K(x,y)\,\d\xi(x)\,\d\xi(y).∥ξ∥K2​:=∬X×X​K(x,y)dξ(x)dξ(y).
(11)

For two probability measures, the maximum mean discrepancy associated with KKK is

MMD⁡K(α,β):=∥α−β∥K.\operatorname{MMD}_K(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \norm{\alpha-\beta}_K.MMDK​(α,β):=∥α−β∥K​.
(12)

These norms are usually called maximum mean discrepancies in statistics and machine learning Gretton et al., 2012Muandet et al., 2017, and kernel norms in shape analysis Hofmann et al., 2008. If X,X′X,X'X,X′ are independent with law α\alphaα, then ∥α∥K2=EX,X′(K(X,X′))\norm{\alpha}_K^2=\EE_{X,X'}(K(X,X'))∥α∥K2​=EX,X′​(K(X,X′)), whenever this expression is finite.

Proposition: Kernel Norm as an RKHS Dual Norm

Let H\mathcal{H}H be the RKHS with reproducing kernel KKK, and assume that the kernel mean embedding

mξ:=∫K(x,⋅)  dξ(x)m_\xi\eqdef\int K(x,\cdot)\,\d\xi(x)mξ​:=∫K(x,⋅)dξ(x)
(13)

is well-defined. Then

∥ξ∥K=sup⁡∥h∥H≤1∫h(x)  dξ(x),\norm{\xi}_K = \sup_{\norm{h}_{\mathcal{H}}\le1} \int h(x)\,\d\xi(x),∥ξ∥K​=∥h∥H​≤1sup​∫h(x)dξ(x),
(14)

so ∥⋅∥K\norm{\cdot}_K∥⋅∥K​ is the dual norm associated with the RKHS unit ball.

Proof

By the reproducing property,

∫h(x)  dξ(x)=⟨h,∫K(x,⋅)  dξ(x)⟩H=⟨h,mξ⟩H.\int h(x)\,\d\xi(x) = \left\langle h,\int K(x,\cdot)\,\d\xi(x) \right\rangle_{\mathcal{H}} = \langle h,m_\xi\rangle_{\mathcal{H}}.∫h(x)dξ(x)=⟨h,∫K(x,⋅)dξ(x)⟩H​=⟨h,mξ​⟩H​.
(15)

Cauchy--Schwarz gives

sup⁡∥h∥H≤1∫h  dξ=∥mξ∥H.\sup_{\norm{h}_{\mathcal{H}}\le1}\int h\,\d\xi = \norm{m_\xi}_{\mathcal{H}}.∥h∥H​≤1sup​∫hdξ=∥mξ​∥H​.
(16)

Finally,

∥mξ∥H2=∬K(x,y)  dξ(x)  dξ(y).\norm{m_\xi}_{\mathcal{H}}^2 = \iint K(x,y)\,\d\xi(x)\,\d\xi(y).∥mξ​∥H2​=∬K(x,y)dξ(x)dξ(y).
(17)
Proposition: Universal Kernels Metrize Weak Convergence

Assume that X\XX is compact and that the RKHS generated by the continuous kernel KKK is dense in C(X)\Cc(\X)C(X) for the uniform norm. Then

MMD⁡K(αn,α)→0⟺αn⇀α\operatorname{MMD}_K(\alpha_n,\alpha)\to0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaMMDK​(αn​,α)→0⟺αn​⇀α
(18)

for probability measures on X\XX.

Proof

If MMD⁡K(αn,α)→0\operatorname{MMD}_K(\alpha_n,\alpha)\to0MMDK​(αn​,α)→0, then integrals of all RKHS functions converge. For any h∈C(X)h\in\Cc(\X)h∈C(X) and any η>0\eta>0η>0, choose g∈Hg\in\mathcal{H}g∈H with ∥h−g∥∞≤η\norm{h-g}_\infty\le\eta∥h−g∥∞​≤η. Since αn\alpha_nαn​ and α\alphaα are probabilities,

∣∫h  d(αn−α)∣≤2η+∣∫g  d(αn−α)∣,\left|\int h\,\d(\alpha_n-\alpha)\right| \le 2\eta + \left|\int g\,\d(\alpha_n-\alpha)\right|,∣∣​∫hd(αn​−α)∣∣​≤2η+∣∣​∫gd(αn​−α)∣∣​,
(19)

and the last term tends to zero. Conversely, if αn⇀α\alpha_n\rightharpoonup\alphaαn​⇀α, then αn⊗αn\alpha_n\otimes\alpha_nαn​⊗αn​, αn⊗α\alpha_n\otimes\alphaαn​⊗α, and α⊗α\alpha\otimes\alphaα⊗α converge weakly on the compact product space. Applying this to the continuous bounded function KKK in

MMD⁡K(αn,α)2=∬K  dαn  dαn−2∬K  dαn  dα+∬K  dα  dα\operatorname{MMD}_K(\alpha_n,\alpha)^2 = \iint K\,\d\alpha_n\,\d\alpha_n -2\iint K\,\d\alpha_n\,\d\alpha +\iint K\,\d\alpha\,\d\alphaMMDK​(αn​,α)2=∬Kdαn​dαn​−2∬Kdαn​dα+∬Kdαdα
(20)

gives convergence to zero.

Further background on RKHS spaces can be found in Berlinet & Thomas-Agnan, 2003Hofmann et al., 2008Schölkopf & Smola, 2002.

Remark: Universal kernels

The hypothesis in Proposition Proposition: Universal Kernels Metrize Weak Convergence is called universality of the kernel. Equivalently, finite sums of the form ∑i=1naik(xi,⋅)\sum_{i=1}^n a_i \Krkhs(x_i,\cdot)∑i=1n​ai​k(xi​,⋅) are dense in C(X)\Cc(\Xx)C(X) for the uniform norm. For translation-invariant kernels on X=Rd\Xx=\RR^dX=Rd, k(x,y)=k0(x−y)\Krkhs(x,y)=\Krkhs_0(x-y)k(x,y)=k0​(x−y), this is equivalent, in the usual sense on compact sets or with suitable decay assumptions, to the Fourier transform not vanishing on its support Sriperumbudur et al., 2008Sriperumbudur et al., 2012.

In the special case where α=∑i=1naiδxi\alpha=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\delta_{x_i}α=∑i=1n​ai​δxi​​ is discrete, one obtains

∥α∥K2=∑i,i′aiai′K(xi,xi′)=a⊤KXa,\norm{\alpha}_K^2 = \sum_{i,i'} a_i a_{i'}K(x_i,x_{i'}) = a^\top K_X a,∥α∥K2​=i,i′∑​ai​ai′​K(xi​,xi′​)=a⊤KX​a,
(21)

where (KX)i,i′=K(xi,xi′)(K_X)_{i,i'}=K(x_i,x_{i'})(KX​)i,i′​=K(xi​,xi′​). In particular, if α=∑iaiδxi\alpha=\sum_i a_i\delta_{x_i}α=∑i​ai​δxi​​ and β=∑ibiδxi\beta=\sum_i b_i\delta_{x_i}β=∑i​bi​δxi​​ are supported on the same point cloud, then ∥α−β∥K2=(a−b)⊤KX(a−b)\norm{\alpha-\beta}_K^2=(a-b)^\top K_X(a-b)∥α−β∥K2​=(a−b)⊤KX​(a−b), a Euclidean quadratic form on the simplex. For two arbitrary discrete measures,

∥α−β∥K2=∑i,i′aiai′K(xi,xi′)+∑j,j′bjbj′K(yj,yj′)−2∑i,jaibjK(xi,yj).\norm{\alpha-\beta}_K^2 = \sum_{i,i'} a_i a_{i'}K(x_i,x_{i'}) + \sum_{j,j'} b_j b_{j'}K(y_j,y_{j'}) - 2\sum_{i,j}a_i b_j K(x_i,y_j).∥α−β∥K2​=i,i′∑​ai​ai′​K(xi​,xi′​)+j,j′∑​bj​bj′​K(yj​,yj′​)−2i,j∑​ai​bj​K(xi​,yj​).
(22)

Phi-Divergences¶

This section develops divergences based on pointwise density ratios. They are computationally simple and statistically classical, but they do not see small spatial displacements between singular measures.

Definition by Density Ratios¶

Phi-divergences are simpler to compute, typically O(n)O(n)O(n) for discrete distributions, but they never metrize weak-∗\ast∗ convergence on singular measures. Another route is possible through Bregman divergences, which may metrize weak-∗\ast∗ convergence when the associated entropy functional is weakly regular.

Definition: Entropy Function

A function ϕ:R→R∪{+∞}\phi:\RR\to\RR\cup\{+\infty\}ϕ:R→R∪{+∞} is an entropy function if it is lower semicontinuous, convex, has domain contained in [0,+∞)[0,+\infty)[0,+∞), and dom⁡ϕ\operatorname{dom}\phidomϕ intersects (0,+∞)(0,+\infty)(0,+∞). Its growth at infinity is described by

ϕ∞′=lim⁡x→+∞ϕ(x)x∈R∪{+∞}.\phi'_\infty = \lim_{x\to+\infty}\frac{\phi(x)}{x} \in \RR\cup\{+\infty\}.ϕ∞′​=x→+∞lim​xϕ(x)​∈R∪{+∞}.
(23)

If ϕ∞′=+∞\phi'_\infty=+\inftyϕ∞′​=+∞, then ϕ\phiϕ grows faster than any linear function and is called superlinear. Any entropy function induces a ϕ\phiϕ-divergence, also known as a Ciszar divergence or fff-divergence Ciszár, 1967Ali & Silvey, 1966.

Definition: ϕ\phiϕ-Divergence

Let ϕ\phiϕ be an entropy function. For α,β∈M(X)\alpha,\beta\in\mathcal{M}(\X)α,β∈M(X), let

α= dα dβ β+α⊥\alpha = \frac{\d\alpha}{\d\beta}\,\beta + \alpha^\perpα=dβdα​β+α⊥
(24)

be the Lebesgue decomposition of α\alphaα with respect to β\betaβ. The divergence is

Dϕ(α∣β):=∫Xϕ( dα dβ)  dβ+ϕ∞′ α⊥(X),D_\phi(\alpha|\beta) \eqdef \int_\X \phi\left(\frac{\d\alpha}{\d\beta}\right) \,\d\beta + \phi'_\infty\,\alpha^\perp(\X),Dϕ​(α∣β):=∫X​ϕ(dβdα​)dβ+ϕ∞′​α⊥(X),
(25)

if α,β\alpha,\betaα,β are nonnegative, and +∞+\infty+∞ otherwise.

Here α⊥\alpha^\perpα⊥ is the part of α\alphaα singular with respect to β\betaβ. The singular term is the recession contribution of the perspective functional. It gives the weak-∗\ast∗ lower-semicontinuous extension of the density-ratio integral when singular mass appears. This is essential for linear-growth entropies such as total variation. For superlinear entropies, such as the usual entropy, ϕ∞′=+∞\phi'_\infty=+\inftyϕ∞′​=+∞, so the divergence is infinite when α\alphaα is not absolutely continuous with respect to β\betaβ.

For discrete measures supported on the same set,

α=∑iaiδxi,β=∑ibiδxi,\alpha=\sum_i a_i\delta_{x_i}, \qquad \beta=\sum_i b_i\delta_{x_i},α=i∑​ai​δxi​​,β=i∑​bi​δxi​​,
(26)

the formula becomes

Dϕ(a∣b)=∑i∈supp⁡(b)bi ϕ(aibi)+ϕ∞′∑i∉supp⁡(b)ai.D_\phi(a|b) = \sum_{i\in\operatorname{supp}(b)} b_i\, \phi\left(\frac{a_i}{b_i}\right) + \phi'_\infty \sum_{i\notin\operatorname{supp}(b)}a_i .Dϕ​(a∣b)=i∈supp(b)∑​bi​ϕ(bi​ai​​)+ϕ∞′​i∈/supp(b)∑​ai​.
(27)
Proposition: Basic Properties of ϕ\phiϕ-Divergences

If ϕ\phiϕ is an entropy function, then DϕD_\phiDϕ​ is jointly 1-homogeneous, convex, and weak-∗\ast∗ lower semicontinuous in (α,β)(\alpha,\beta)(α,β).

Proof

Define the perspective

ψ(u,v)={v ϕ(u/v),v>0,u ϕ∞′,v=0.\psi(u,v) = \begin{cases} v\,\phi(u/v), & v>0,\\ u\,\phi'_\infty, & v=0. \end{cases}ψ(u,v)={vϕ(u/v),uϕ∞′​,​v>0,v=0.​
(28)

Joint 1-homogeneity follows directly. In the discrete case, Dϕ(a∣b)=∑iψ(ai,bi)D_\phi(a|b)=\sum_i\psi(a_i,b_i)Dϕ​(a∣b)=∑i​ψ(ai​,bi​), so it is enough to show that ψ\psiψ is convex. For v1,v2>0v_1,v_2>0v1​,v2​>0, λ∈[0,1]\lambda\in[0,1]λ∈[0,1], τ=1−λ\tau=1-\lambdaτ=1−λ, set

θ1=τv1τv1+λv2,θ2=λv2τv1+λv2.\theta_1=\frac{\tau v_1}{\tau v_1+\lambda v_2}, \qquad \theta_2=\frac{\lambda v_2}{\tau v_1+\lambda v_2}.θ1​=τv1​+λv2​τv1​​,θ2​=τv1​+λv2​λv2​​.
(29)

Then θ1+θ2=1\theta_1+\theta_2=1θ1​+θ2​=1 and

τu1+λu2τv1+λv2=θ1u1v1+θ2u2v2.\frac{\tau u_1+\lambda u_2}{\tau v_1+\lambda v_2} = \theta_1\frac{u_1}{v_1} + \theta_2\frac{u_2}{v_2}.τv1​+λv2​τu1​+λu2​​=θ1​v1​u1​​+θ2​v2​u2​​.
(30)

Convexity of ϕ\phiϕ gives convexity of ψ\psiψ on v>0v>0v>0; the case v=0v=0v=0 follows by lower semicontinuity of the recession value. In the measure case, weak-∗\ast∗ lower semicontinuity is the standard theorem for convex integral functionals with recession extension.

Proposition: Nonnegativity of ϕ\phiϕ-Divergences

Assume that ϕ(1)=0\phi(1)=0ϕ(1)=0. For probability measures, Dϕ(α∣β)≥0D_\phi(\alpha|\beta)\ge0Dϕ​(α∣β)≥0. If ϕ\phiϕ is strictly convex, then equality holds if and only if α=β\alpha=\betaα=β. This extends to arbitrary nonnegative measures if one also imposes ϕ≥0\phi\ge0ϕ≥0.

Proof

Let m=α+βm=\alpha+\betam=α+β and write a= dα/ dma=\d\alpha/\d ma=dα/dm, b= dβ/ dmb=\d\beta/\d mb=dβ/dm. Using the perspective,

Dϕ(α∣β)=∫ψ(a,b)  dm.D_\phi(\alpha|\beta) = \int \psi(a,b)\,\d m.Dϕ​(α∣β)=∫ψ(a,b)dm.
(31)

For probability measures, ∫a  dm=∫b  dm=1\int a\,\d m=\int b\,\d m=1∫adm=∫bdm=1. Jensen’s inequality and ψ(1,1)=ϕ(1)=0\psi(1,1)=\phi(1)=0ψ(1,1)=ϕ(1)=0 give

Dϕ(α∣β)≥ψ(∫a  dm,∫b  dm)=0.D_\phi(\alpha|\beta) \ge \psi\left(\int a\,\d m,\int b\,\d m\right) =0.Dϕ​(α∣β)≥ψ(∫adm,∫bdm)=0.
(32)

If ϕ\phiϕ is strictly convex, equality in Jensen forces a=ba=ba=b almost everywhere, hence α=β\alpha=\betaα=β.

Classical Examples and Topology¶

The following examples calibrate the strength of ϕ\phiϕ-divergences. KL is sensitive to absolute continuity, while total variation gives the strong topology and therefore behaves very differently from Wasserstein-type weak metrics.

Example: Kullback--Leibler divergence

The Kullback--Leibler divergence KL⁡:=DϕKL⁡\KL \eqdef \Divergm_{\phi_{\KL}}KL:=DϕKL​​, also known as the relative entropy, was already introduced in (20) and (11). It is the divergence associated to the Shannon--Boltzmann entropy function ϕKL⁡\phi_{\KL}ϕKL​, given by

ϕKL⁡(s)={slog⁡(s)−s+1for s>0,1for s=0,+∞otherwise.\phi_{\KL}(s)= \begin{cases} s\log(s)-s+1 & \textnormal{for } s>0 , \\ 1 & \textnormal{for } s=0 , \\ +\infty & \textnormal{otherwise.} \end{cases}ϕKL​(s)=⎩⎨⎧​slog(s)−s+11+∞​for s>0,for s=0,otherwise.​
(33)
Example: Total variation

The total variation distance TV:=DϕTV\TV \eqdef \Divergm_{\phi_{\TV}}TV:=DϕTV​​ is the divergence associated to

ϕTV(s)={∣s−1∣for s≥0,+∞otherwise.\phi_{\TV}(s)= \begin{cases} |s-1| & \textnormal{for } s\geq0 , \\ +\infty & \textnormal{otherwise.} \end{cases}ϕTV​(s)={∣s−1∣+∞​for s≥0,otherwise.​
(34)

It actually defines a norm on the full space of measures M(X)\Mm(\X)M(X) where

TV(α∣β)=∥α−β∥TV,where∥α∥TV=∣α∣(X)=∫X d∣α∣(x).\TV(\al|\be) = \norm{\al-\be}_{\TV}, \qquad\text{where}\qquad \norm{\al}_{\TV} = |\al|(\X) = \int_\X \d|\al|(x).TV(α∣β)=∥α−β∥TV​,where∥α∥TV​=∣α∣(X)=∫X​d∣α∣(x).
(35)

If α\alα has a density ρα\density{\al}ρα​ on X=Rd\X=\RR^\dimX=Rd, then the TV norm is the L1L^1L1 norm on functions, ∥α∥TV=∫X∣ρα(x)∣ dx=∥ρα∥L1\norm{\al}_{\TV} = \int_\X |\density{\al}(x)| \d x = \norm{\density{\al}}_{L^1}∥α∥TV​=∫X​∣ρα​(x)∣dx=∥ρα​∥L1​.

If α\alα is discrete as in (27), then the TV norm is the ℓ1\ell^1ℓ1 norm of vectors in Rn\RR^nRn, ∥α∥TV=∑i∣ai∣=∥a∥ℓ1\norm{\al}_{\TV}=\sum_i |\a_i| = \norm{\a}_{\ell^1}∥α∥TV​=∑i​∣ai​∣=∥a∥ℓ1​.

Remark: Strong vs. weak topology

The total variation norm (35) defines the so-called “strong” topology on the space of measures.

On a compact domain X\XX of radius RRR, one has

W1(α,β)≤R∥α−β∥TV\Wass_1(\al,\be) \leq R \norm{\al-\be}_{\TV}W1​(α,β)≤R∥α−β∥TV​
(36)

so that this strong notion of convergence implies the weak convergence metrized by Wasserstein distances.

The converse is, however, not true, since δx\de_xδx​ does not converge strongly to δy\de_yδy​ if x→yx \rightarrow yx→y (note that ∥δx−δy∥TV=2\norm{\de_x-\de_y}_{\TV}=2∥δx​−δy​∥TV​=2 if x≠yx \neq yx=y).

A chief advantage is that M+1(X)\Mm_+^1(\Xx)M+1​(X) (once again on a compact ground space X\XX) is compact for the weak topology so that from any sequence of probability measures (αk)k(\al_k)_k(αk​)k​, one can always extract a converging subsequence, which makes it a suitable space for several optimization problems.

Main Families of ϕ\phiϕ-Divergences¶

Several classical divergences fit in the same template. The power-divergence family

ϕγ(s)=sγ−γs+γ−1γ(γ−1)(γ≠0,1)\phi_\gamma(s) = \frac{s^\gamma-\gamma s+\gamma-1}{\gamma(\gamma-1)} \qquad(\gamma\ne0,1)ϕγ​(s)=γ(γ−1)sγ−γs+γ−1​(γ=0,1)
(37)

interpolates between the Pearson χ2\chi^2χ2 divergence at γ=2\gamma=2γ=2, a Hellinger-type behavior at γ=1/2\gamma=1/2γ=1/2, and, by taking limits, the KL divergence as γ→1\gamma\to1γ→1 and the reverse KL or Burg entropy ϕ0(s)=−log⁡s+s−1\phi_0(s)=-\log s+s-1ϕ0​(s)=−logs+s−1 as γ→0\gamma\to0γ→0. The Hellinger divergence is often written with ϕH(s)=(s−1)2\phi_H(s)=(\sqrt{s}-1)^2ϕH​(s)=(s​−1)2; for measures with densities, Hellinger⁡(α,β)=∥ρα−ρβ∥L2\operatorname{Hellinger}(\alpha,\beta) =\norm{\sqrt{\rho_\alpha}-\sqrt{\rho_\beta}}_{L^2}Hellinger(α,β)=∥∥​ρα​​−ρβ​​∥∥​L2​. The Jensen--Shannon divergence is the symmetrized and bounded KL-to-the-mixture divergence

JS⁡(α,β)2=12KL⁡ ⁣(α|α+β2)+12KL⁡ ⁣(β|α+β2),\operatorname{JS}(\alpha,\beta)^2 = \frac12\operatorname{KL}\!\left(\alpha\middle|\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right) + \frac12\operatorname{KL}\!\left(\beta\middle|\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right),JS(α,β)2=21​KL(α∣∣​2α+β​)+21​KL(β∣∣​2α+β​),
(38)

generated, up to an irrelevant affine term, by ϕJS⁡(s)=slog⁡s−(s+1)log⁡((s+1)/2)\phi_{\operatorname{JS}}(s)=s\log s-(s+1)\log((s+1)/2)ϕJS​(s)=slogs−(s+1)log((s+1)/2). Total variation, generated by ∣s−1∣|s-1|∣s−1∣, is exceptional because it is both a ϕ\phiϕ-divergence and an integral probability metric.

show_book_figure("dualnorms-phi-generators")

ϕ\phiϕ-divergences through density ratios. The left panel shows normalized generators for common divergences as functions of s= dα/ dβs=\d\alpha/\d\betas=dα/dβ; all curves vanish at s=1s=1s=1 up to affine normalization. The right panel shows the discrete formula Dϕ(a∣b)=∑ibiϕ(ai/bi)D_\phi(a|b)=\sum_i b_i\phi(a_i/b_i)Dϕ​(a∣b)=∑i​bi​ϕ(ai​/bi​): hollow blue circles encode bib_ibi​, filled red circles encode aia_iai​, the violet curve gives the ratios ai/bia_i/b_iai​/bi​, and orange lollipops show local KL-type contributions.

The interactive demo changes the generator family and the amount of mismatch between two discrete histograms. The near-zero control deliberately creates small target bins, making the recession and singularity behavior visible: ratio-based penalties react to overlap and density ratios rather than to spatial displacement.

Interactive panel. Use the divergence and ratio controls to compare convex generators and their dual penalties around density ratio one.

Remark: ϕ\phiϕ-divergences versus Bregman divergences

Except for KL-type entropies, ϕ\phiϕ-divergences should not be confused with Bregman divergences. A ϕ\phiϕ-divergence compares measures pointwise through the density ratio  dα/ dβ\d\alpha/\d\betadα/dβ and is invariant under measurable changes of variables. A Bregman divergence is generated by a convex functional on a linear space and compares two points through first-order Taylor error. KL is special because the integral entropy α↦∫ρlog⁡ρ\alpha\mapsto\int \rho\log\rhoα↦∫ρlogρ produces a Bregman divergence whose density-ratio form is also a ϕ\phiϕ-divergence.

Variational Dual Formula¶

The following formula turns a pointwise density-ratio penalty into a dual optimization problem over test functions. It is the analogue, for ϕ\phiϕ-divergences, of the Kantorovich dual formula for transport costs.

Proposition: Dual Expression for ϕ\phiϕ-Divergences

A ϕ\phiϕ-divergence can be expressed using the positive-domain Legendre transform

ϕ∗,≥0(s):=sup⁡t≥0 st−ϕ(t)\phi^{*,\ge0}(s) \eqdef \sup_{t\ge0}\, st-\phi(t)ϕ∗,≥0(s):=t≥0sup​st−ϕ(t)
(39)

as

Dϕ(α∣β)=sup⁡f{∫Xf(x)  dα(x)−∫Xϕ∗,≥0(f(x))  dβ(x)}.D_\phi(\alpha|\beta) = \sup_f \left\{ \int_\X f(x)\,\d\alpha(x) - \int_\X \phi^{*,\ge0}(f(x))\,\d\beta(x) \right\}.Dϕ​(α∣β)=fsup​{∫X​f(x)dα(x)−∫X​ϕ∗,≥0(f(x))dβ(x)}.
(40)

Equivalently,

Dϕ∗(f∣β)=∫Xϕ∗,≥0(f(x))  dβ(x).D_\phi^*(f|\beta) = \int_\X \phi^{*,\ge0}(f(x))\,\d\beta(x).Dϕ∗​(f∣β)=∫X​ϕ∗,≥0(f(x))dβ(x).
(41)
Proof

First assume ϕ∞′=+∞\phi'_\infty=+\inftyϕ∞′​=+∞, so the divergence is infinite unless α\alphaα has a density ρ≥0\rho\ge0ρ≥0 with respect to β\betaβ. The Legendre--Fenchel transform of Dϕ(⋅∣β)D_\phi(\cdot|\beta)Dϕ​(⋅∣β) is

Dϕ∗(f∣β)=sup⁡ρ≥0∫Xf(x)ρ(x)  dβ(x)−∫Xϕ(ρ(x))  dβ(x)=∫Xsup⁡ρ(x)≥0(f(x)ρ(x)−ϕ(ρ(x))) dβ(x).D_\phi^*(f|\beta) = \sup_{\rho\ge0} \int_\X f(x)\rho(x)\,\d\beta(x) - \int_\X\phi(\rho(x))\,\d\beta(x) = \int_\X \sup_{\rho(x)\ge0} \left(f(x)\rho(x)-\phi(\rho(x))\right) \d\beta(x).Dϕ∗​(f∣β)=ρ≥0sup​∫X​f(x)ρ(x)dβ(x)−∫X​ϕ(ρ(x))dβ(x)=∫X​ρ(x)≥0sup​(f(x)ρ(x)−ϕ(ρ(x)))dβ(x).
(42)

This is the displayed integral of ϕ∗,≥0\phi^{*,\ge0}ϕ∗,≥0. Fenchel--Moreau gives the dual expression. For a general entropy, the same argument is applied to the perspective with its recession term; the singular part is encoded by the effective domain of ϕ∗,≥0\phi^{*,\ge0}ϕ∗,≥0.

GANs via Duality¶

GANs fit naturally into the dual viewpoint: the discriminator is a parameterized potential and the generator moves a reference measure. This section first explains the original divergence-based GAN objective, then contrasts it with integral probability metrics such as MMD and Wasserstein distances.

The goal is to fit a generative parametric model αθ=(gθ)♯ζ\alpha_\theta=(g_\theta)_\sharp\zetaαθ​=(gθ​)♯​ζ to empirical data

β=1m∑jδyj,\beta=\frac1m\sum_j\delta_{y_j},β=m1​j∑​δyj​​,
(43)

where ζ\zetaζ is a fixed density over the latent space and gθ:Z→Xg_\theta:\mathcal{Z}\to\Xgθ​:Z→X is the generator, often a neural network.

Divergence-Based Adversarial Losses¶

Any ϕ\phiϕ-divergence can be written in adversarial form through the dual formula:

min⁡θDϕ(αθ∣β)=min⁡θsup⁡f{∫Xf  dαθ−Dϕ∗(f∣β)}=min⁡θsup⁡f{∫Zf(gθ(z))  dζ(z)−1m∑jϕ∗(f(yj))}.\min_\theta D_\phi(\alpha_\theta|\beta) = \min_\theta\sup_f \left\{ \int_\X f\,\d\alpha_\theta - D_\phi^*(f|\beta) \right\} = \min_\theta\sup_f \left\{ \int_\mathcal{Z} f(g_\theta(z))\,\d\zeta(z) - \frac1m\sum_j\phi^*(f(y_j)) \right\}.θmin​Dϕ​(αθ​∣β)=θmin​fsup​{∫X​fdαθ​−Dϕ∗​(f∣β)}=θmin​fsup​{∫Z​f(gθ​(z))dζ(z)−m1​j∑​ϕ∗(f(yj​))}.
(44)

Replacing the unrestricted potential fff by a neural network fξf_\xifξ​ gives a saddle problem

min⁡θmax⁡ξ∫Zfξ(gθ(z))  dζ(z)−1m∑jϕ∗(fξ(yj)).\min_\theta\max_\xi \int_\mathcal{Z} f_\xi(g_\theta(z))\,\d\zeta(z) - \frac1m\sum_j\phi^*(f_\xi(y_j)).θmin​ξmax​∫Z​fξ​(gθ​(z))dζ(z)−m1​j∑​ϕ∗(fξ​(yj​)).
(45)

The original vanilla GAN Goodfellow et al., 2014 is this construction for the Jensen--Shannon generator

ϕJS⁡(s)=slog⁡s−(s+1)log⁡s+12,ϕJS⁡∗(u)=−log⁡(2−eu),u<log⁡2,\phi_{\operatorname{JS}}(s) = s\log s-(s+1)\log\frac{s+1}{2}, \qquad \phi_{\operatorname{JS}}^*(u) = -\log(2-e^u), \quad u<\log2,ϕJS​(s)=slogs−(s+1)log2s+1​,ϕJS∗​(u)=−log(2−eu),u<log2,
(46)

up to affine normalizations and the usual reparametrization of the potential by a discriminator with values in (0,1)(0,1)(0,1). In practice the min--max problem is solved by alternating stochastic gradient descent/ascent. Unlike the convex-concave variational formula, the neural parametrization is nonconvex in θ\thetaθ and nonconcave in ξ\xiξ, which explains instability and mode-collapse pathologies. These losses estimate density ratios, which is meaningful when the measures overlap but can saturate when the model and data are mutually singular. For example, the Jensen--Shannon divergence is already maximal for disjoint supports.

Dual Norms and Integral Probability Metrics¶

Instead of a density-ratio divergence, one can minimize an integral probability metric:

min⁡θ∥αθ−β∥B=min⁡θsup⁡f∈B{∫Zf(gθ(z))  dζ(z)−1m∑jf(yj)}.\min_\theta\norm{\alpha_\theta-\beta}_B = \min_\theta \sup_{f\in B} \left\{ \int_\mathcal{Z} f(g_\theta(z))\,\d\zeta(z) - \frac1m\sum_j f(y_j) \right\}.θmin​∥αθ​−β∥B​=θmin​f∈Bsup​{∫Z​f(gθ​(z))dζ(z)−m1​j∑​f(yj​)}.
(47)

MMD-GANs take BBB to be a unit ball in an RKHS Dziugaite et al., 2015; Wasserstein GANs take BBB to be a Lipschitz ball, following Kantorovich--Rubinstein duality Arjovsky et al., 2017Frogner et al., 2015. The advantage is topological: for bounded continuous RKHS balls, or for bounded Lipschitz balls on compact spaces, the objective is weakly continuous. It can therefore compare singular empirical and generated measures through test functions instead of requiring pointwise density ratios. The price is that the discriminator class must be controlled geometrically, either by a kernel norm, a Lipschitz constraint, or a related regularization.

Wasserstein GANs originally used weight clipping as a proxy for enforcing fξ∈{f:Lip⁡(f)≤1}f_\xi\in\{f:\operatorname{Lip}(f)\le1\}fξ​∈{f:Lip(f)≤1}. This parameter set is both smaller than the true Lipschitz ball and nonconvex, so clipping should be understood as a practical heuristic rather than a faithful implementation of the Kantorovich--Rubinstein dual constraint.

Remark: Weight clipping is only a proxy

Wasserstein GANs originally used weight clipping, constraining ∥ξ∥∞≤1\norm{\xi}_\infty \leq 1∥ξ∥∞​≤1 as a proxy for enforcing fξ∈B={f  :  Lip⁡(f)≤1}\f_\xi \in B = \enscond{f}{\Lip(f) \leq 1}fξ​∈B={f:Lip(f)≤1}. This parameter set is both smaller than the true Lipschitz ball and non-convex, so clipping should be understood as a practical heuristic rather than a faithful implementation of the Kantorovich--Rubinstein dual constraint.

  3. Sriperumbudur, B. K., Fukumizu, K., Gretton, A., Schölkopf, B., & Lanckriet, G. R. (2009). On integral probability metrics, ϕ-divergences and binary classification. arXiv Preprint arXiv:0901.2698.
  4. Goodfellow, I., Pouget-Abadie, J., Mirza, M., Xu, B., Warde-Farley, D., Ozair, S., Courville, A., & Bengio, Y. (2014). Generative adversarial nets. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2672–2680.
  5. Arjovsky, M., Chintala, S., & Bottou, L. (2017). Wasserstein generative adversarial networks. In D. Precup & Y. W. Teh (Eds.), Proceedings of the 34th International Conference on Machine Learning (Vol. 70, pp. 214–223). PMLR. https://proceedings.mlr.press/v70/arjovsky17a.html
  6. Sriperumbudur, B. K., Fukumizu, K., Gretton, A., Schölkopf, B., & Lanckriet, G. R. (2012). On the empirical estimation of integral probability metrics. Electronic Journal of Statistics, 6, 1550–1599.
  7. Sriperumbudur, B. K., Gretton, A., Fukumizu, K., Lanckriet, G., & Schölkopf, B. (2008). Injective Hilbert space embeddings of probability measures. Proceedings of the 21st Annual Conference on Learning Theory, 111–122.
  8. Hanin, L. G. (1992). Kantorovich-Rubinstein norm and its application in the theory of Lipschitz spaces. Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society, 115(2), 345–352.
  9. Lellmann, J., Lorenz, D. A., Schönlieb, C., & Valkonen, T. (2014). Imaging with Kantorovich–Rubinstein discrepancy. SIAM Journal on Imaging Sciences, 7(4), 2833–2859.
  10. Berg, C., Christensen, J. P. R., & Ressel, P. (1984). Harmonic Analysis on Semigroups. Springer Verlag.
  11. Schoenberg, I. J. (1938). Metric spaces and positive definite functions. Transactions of the American Mathematical Society, 44(3), 522–536. 10.1090/S0002-9947-1938-1501980-0
  12. Székely, G. J., & Rizzo, M. L. (2004). Testing for equal distributions in high dimension. InterStat, 5(16.10).
  13. Wendland, H. (2005). Scattered Data Approximation. Cambridge University Press.
  14. Gretton, A., Borgwardt, K. M., Rasch, M. J., Schölkopf, B., & Smola, A. (2012). A kernel two-sample test. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 13(Mar), 723–773.
  15. Muandet, K., Fukumizu, K., Sriperumbudur, B., & Schölkopf, B. (2017). Kernel mean embedding of distributions: a review and beyond. Foundations and Trends in Machine Learning, 10(1–2), 1–141.
